name: Trunko
description: Trunko refers to an alleged cryptid sighted in Margate, South Africa, in 1924. Eyewitnesses described a large, white, woolly creature, reportedly resembling a polar bear or elephant seal, engaging in a battle with two killer whales off the coast. The creature, estimated to be around 47 feet long, was said to have propelled itself out of the water with a large, trunk-like appendage, hence the name 'Trunko.' Proponents suggest Trunko was a previously unknown marine species or a surviving member of a prehistoric whale lineage. The primary evidence consists of eyewitness accounts and a single photograph of what appears to be a beached carcass. Mainstream science generally dismisses Trunko as a misidentification of a known marine animal, such as a whale carcass in an advanced state of decomposition, or a hoax. The lack of physical evidence beyond anecdotal reports makes it difficult to verify Trunko's existence. Skeptics highlight the potential for misinterpretation in eyewitness accounts, especially in unusual or stressful situations. The 'woolly' appearance is often attributed to the sloughing off of skin and blubber from a decaying marine animal. The 'trunk' is likely a severely decomposed tail section or other body part. While the story has captured the imagination of cryptozoologists, it lacks the scientific rigor required for acceptance as a legitimate zoological phenomenon.
subtitle: Woolly sea monster of Margate, South Africa

claims.claim: Trunko was an undiscovered marine species.
claims.evidence: Eyewitness accounts describing a unique 'woolly' creature with a trunk.
claims.criticism: Eyewitness accounts are subjective and prone to error. The 'woolly' appearance likely results from decomposition.

claims.claim: Trunko was a surviving member of a prehistoric whale lineage.
claims.evidence: Resemblance to descriptions of ancient whale species.
claims.criticism: The resemblance is superficial and based on limited information. No fossil evidence supports this claim.

claims.claim: The 'trunk' was a functional appendage used for locomotion.
claims.evidence: Eyewitness reports of the creature propelling itself out of the water with the 'trunk'.
claims.criticism: The 'trunk' was more likely a decomposed tail section or other body part manipulated by wave action.

keyFigures.name: Ivan T. Sanderson
keyFigures.role: Cryptozoologist who popularized the Trunko story

keyFigures.name: Darren Naish
keyFigures.role: Zoologist and science communicator who has critiqued the Trunko claims

timeline.event: Alleged Trunko sighting in Margate, South Africa
Reports of the sighting began to circulate in newspapers and magazines

sources.author: Karl Shuker
sources.work: The Beasts That Hide From Man
sources.title: Karl Shuker
